description: Modern Slavery Bill New Zealand Ethical Employers Incorporated Mike Chapman, Chair NZ Ethical Employers Kerri Ahomiro, Executive Office NZ Ethical Employers Wellington Community Justice Project Sam Collier Redstone Consulting Brown Makatesa Brian Ward Oceana Gold (New Zealand) Limited Jackie St John, Manager Legal Kylie Stevens, Legal Counsel World Vision New Zealand and World Vision NZ (Advocacy Ambassadors) [Joint submission] Rebekah Armstrong, Evelyn Paynter The Warehouse Group Phil Cumming Ponsonby Chambers Tanya Dunstan Brent Wilton Jane Carrigan Max Marshall Business Council for Sustainable Development Australia Andrew Petersen PSA ALMA Network and PSA Youth Network [joint hearing] Pasang Sherpa Te Pukenga Here Tikanga
descriptionSummary: Modern Slavery Bill. New Zealand Ethical Employers Incorporated. Mike Chapman, Chair NZ Ethical Employers

chunks.text: Hopefully both are available and ready.

Welcome both. Thank you. Kerry just needs to turn the camera and microphone on. But in the meantime, we've got 10 minutes with you this afternoon. That includes time for questions, and I'll hand it over to you now. Thank you. My name is Mike, as you know, and I'm the chair of New Zealand Deaf Employers. Kerry is our executive officer.

And what our members do is we put the human before the system. Our members employ about 25,000 workers, 20% are full-time, 80% are seasonal, and out of the 80% that are seasonal, about half are offshore workers. So our submission comes from our experience and our members' experience with the employment of offshore workers and trying to change the dynamic of how things work in horticulture, wine grapes, and processing.

We support the bill, but we very much think it needs to be strengthened. There is not enough accountability in our view. It must be more than a paperwork exercise, and to us, it looks a bit like a paperwork exercise at present. If something happens, if there's a credible risk or incident, there needs to be a progression of responses that are mandatory and that employers need to take. To explain a little bit more, I'll hand over to Kerry.

Thanks, Mike. One of the things that we've included in our submission is in relation to worker voice. There's a common assumption that low complaint numbers mean low risk, and in our experience, that's not always the case. Migrant and seasonal workers can be particularly reluctant to raise concerns for a whole range of reasons. But if workers don't feel safe speaking up, then some of the highest risk issues will never appear in a report, audit, or an annual statement.

And that's why we believe worker voice systems need to be a trusted, safe, culturally appropriate, and accessible in a worker's own language. And at NZDE, we provide those systems to our members, not because... Excuse me. At NZDE, we provide our members with those support systems because we know that hearing directly from workers often provides insights that aren't visible through compliance reporting alone.

And the second point that we'd like to make is around hidden risks. So the bill creates visibility, which is really important, and we do support it, but visibility alone won't protect or detect modern-day slavery unless there are systems around it that help identify concerns, hear from the workers, respond to the risks, and support the enforcement. And some of the highest risk practices come from smaller, less visible operators further down the supply chain.

And in many of those cases, the people engaging in exploitive practices are also very good at avoiding scrutiny. So reporting entities shouldn't become regulators, but they do need a clear referral and escalation pathway where serious concerns are identified, and that's where we think the bill could be strengthened.

So there needs to be a clear understanding of who should be notified, what action is expected, how workers will be protected, and how serious issues will be addressed. And the final point that I'd like to make comes from my own previous experience working in the Labour Inspectorate. I spent 16 years in the Labour Inspectorate.

And the biggest message there is good systems are most effective when they're easy to understand. We need to avoid creating a framework that simply pushes compliance obligations down onto the smaller employers through reporting requests. And many businesses, including NZDE members, already operate under detailed employment, immigration, and worker welfare requirements.

NZDE members are already undertaking human rights due diligence because it's the right thing to do. So where credible systems already exist, they should be recognized. And we also encourage the development of sector-specific guidance and standardized templates so that businesses can focus their efforts on identifying and managing genuine risk rather than duplicating paperwork.

So to conclude, we absolutely support the bill. We support greater transparency and visibility across the supply chains, and transparency gives us visibility, but visibility alone won't protect the workers. So for the bill to be effective, it needs to reward substance over paperwork, and that means providing clear guidance for businesses, ensuring there are clear pathways for escalation when serious concerns do arise, supporting worker voice systems, and requiring reasonable action where credible risks are identified. And with those changes, we think

that this will help turn transparency into better outcomes for workers. Thank you. A great package by Kerry there. Fantastic. Mike, were you rounding off, or are we open for questions? No, open for questions. Great. Nothing better- We've got one from Camilla Belich. Thank you both for the work you're doing for submitting on this bill.

Just two questions really. So you've talked a lot about clear expectations for employers. We've had a lot of submissions around due diligence, which is likely something that other countries are seeking to implement in their regimes. And I also just wanted to clarify, you made some quite detailed oral submissions and also written submissions around duplication. And I just wanted to probe that a wee bit, if I may, in that at the moment, there isn't a mandatory requirement for large companies to report on their supply

chains. And, so what is the kind of the issue with duplication that you think-What's the problem that-- I appreciate that you said there will be duplication, but what's the problem that you are seeking to raise with us in respect of that? Kerry? I think possibly duplication might not be quite the right word, but I know for our members who operate, particularly when they're operating under some of the immigration schemes, they're already heavily audited.

And so there's already a whole lot of reporting that goes on. So I think in my mind, if we have a human rights structure that already recognizes some of those systems, rather than having to create a whole new process just for the worker boys, for the modern-day slavery regime, if we can look at developing something that recognizes those systems are already in existence and what that might mean, particularly around the auditing that goes on in those processes.

And the first question, Kerry. Which was Katie, sorry, Camilla- Sorry... what was your first question again? Sorry, Camilla. I just wanted to know if you supported the due diligence- Oh... requirements that- Absolutely. Yeah. Absolutely support, sorry.

Great. And we have time for a question from Dr. Parmjeet Parmar. Thank you. A very quick clarification from you about due diligence, and you also spoke about how far you go in the supply chain, in a multi-tier supply chain. And want to understand, for due diligence, whose obligation it should be because if you're relying on that overseas company, basically you're relying on some paperwork that that company will produce after the audit is done or whatever process is adopted.

So how do you think that the business here can be 100% sure about that due diligence, and how do you see it working? I think for the incoming due diligence, so if we're talking about receiving product from overseas suppliers, that's quite a different focus to NZEU's focus, which is on the outgoing. So, most of our employers are SMEs who won't be captured by the proposed reporting levels at the moment, but they will be captured in the

downstream. So those employers who will be required to report will be seeking further information from the smaller SMEs in their chains, and that's where our concern around duplication came from. Mike, do you want to talk to the- Yeah... other side?

So one of the things we do with our members, because they recruit offshore, is give them a contract to use for if they use anyone recruiting offshore and some checklists which they can use. And so they become intimately involved in what is happening offshore. They just don't take the word for it or a bit of paper. It requires an active involvement, and that probably involves going to that country and ensuring that things are being done properly. Because all the workers who come into New Zealand, there's an enormous risk that they've paid some amazing amount of

money to someone to actually get the opportunity to come to New Zealand when they don't have to pay anything. So what we try and do is equip our members with the tools and the ability to look into that and to stop it, because that's one of the worst exploitations of workers coming to New Zealand. But same with supply of goods. You cannot rely on the bit of paper that you get from someone offshore.

You actually have to go and look. And if you're part of an organization like ours, we can go and look for everyone, if you know what I mean. It doesn't mean that each importer of goods has to go and do it himself or herself. There can be processes put in place, and even government with embassies and high commissions can help with that if they are required to, if they don't mind.

Thank you very much, both of you. We've reached the end of our time with you this afternoon, but really appreciate your participation in the process. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. And we'll move on now to our next submitter. We have Wellington Community Justice Project, Sam Collier speaking on behalf.

Welcome, Sam. We have 10 minutes with you this afternoon. That includes time for questions. We'll hand over to you now. Sweet. All good. Yeah. Kia ora koutou. My name is Sam. I'm a fourth year law student at Te Herenga Waka—Victoria University of Wellington, representing the Wellington Community Justice Project as part of the law reform team. I also have here with me Finn Studd, also part of the Wellington Community Justice Project as well.

So I might move, I don't think the lighting is the best. It's okay. We can still see you, still hear you. It's good. Sweet. All good. I guess in this submission, I'll cover two key points. Firstly, the bill's highly lenient offense provisions and kind of the ad hoc regulatory framework fails to hold some reporting entities accountable where they have engaged or may be likely to engage in modern slavery practices.

And secondly, the absence of mandatory due diligence requirements neutralizes the kind of systemic and kind of abhorrent nature of modern slavery through creating a kind of illusion of accountability for some of the companies. I'd also like to talk about a third point that-The absence of victim compensatory mechanisms within the bill kind of exacerbates the harm experienced by some individuals who are most impacted by modern slavery practices within operations and supply chains.

But yeah, I guess my overall kind of position is it should not proceed as stands, and the provisions should be subject to amendment or omission altogether. Yeah. Yeah. Did you have anyone with you there that wanted to speak? Sorry, Sam. Yes, Finn, but later, if that's all good.

Oh, yeah. No, go for it. Keep going. Yeah. So yeah, I guess the first kind of point I would like to talk about was the provisions fail to hold the commercial entities accountable. As I talked about in my written submission, the maximum penalty for a reporting entity failing to prepare and publish inaccurately prescribed modern slavery statement is 600,000, which is quite disproportionate to the annual revenue of many companies.

Obviously, I talked about the top three kind of companies in New Zealand. Their average annual revenue it was about 15 billion. So it's like the financial disincentives are unlikely to meaningfully deter entities and their directors. But yeah, also modeled on Australia's Modern Slavery Act, the bill provides that only entities with a consolidated revenue of at least 100 million can be subject to liability.

So, as I talked about with Gloriavale as one such example, their annual revenue was about 20.3 million in the last kind of financial year. But it's not confined to such entities. Various other companies have been demonstrated to do this. An example is the now liquidated kiwifruit contracting company, Harry Ng Horticulture Limited. They were ordered to pay $40,000 in penalties by the Employment Relations Authority for exploiting migrant workers by failing to comply with minimum employment standards.

They also required one worker to pay a premium of $23,000 simply to secure employment. Another example is Rural Practice Limited, a dairy farming company. They were ordered to pay $215,000 in penalties by the Employment Levels Authority after being found to have exploited three Indonesian migrant workers.

The head compliance enforcement labor inspector also said the case was illustrative of deliberate and systemic exploitation. The other point I would like to talk about, as mentioned, was the ad hoc regulatory framework. It has been known that such ad hoc kind of regulation is counter to the rule of law.

I think, as the bill's preventative purpose is to reduce modern slavery incidents and raise public awareness, I think that kind of counteracts that and potentially, yeah, I think the threshold revenue should be omitted, and until further explanation has been provided for such definition. I'll now have Finn to talk about the due diligence requirements.

Sorry. Hello, so I'm just going to briefly talk about due diligence. So I think the first main thing is discretion to act with due diligence might exacerbate the likelihood of corporate malpractice. So section 9 (2) of the bill obviously has modern slavery, requires reporting of modern slavery, but not limited to due diligence and remediation, but there's no legal obligation to act with due diligence. It might just seem like a bit of an arbitrary standard, possibly. It might also set a precedent that you can report and continue engaging, but they can be

protected under some, well, I don't know if it's an illusion, but without as much accountability as you'd probably like for something of this caliber. Next thing is the international metrics are not proportionate for New Zealand. So New Zealand obviously has, this bill has this standard, but other countries are a little bit more vigilant of, I guess. So like France's Duty of Vigilance law, so it improves quality of reporting, in human rights compliance by the 20 largest French companies.

If we take that the bill's purpose is to raise public awareness of modern slavery, it would probably be better to impose a legal obligation on entities to undertake the due diligence for reporting. And the Human Rights Commission also recommended, furthermore, despite the Human Rights Commission's recommendation, there's no ratification of the International Convention of the Protection of Rights of All Migrant Workers and Members of Their Families, but over 40 countries are members of that, and they're signatories, further 20 being

parties. And so then, I guess lastly, do you want to talk about that? Yeah. So the last point I talked about in the kind of summary that I did not talk about in my written submissions was that the absence of kind of any victim compensatory mechanism within the bill exacerbates the kind of harm experienced by those most impacted.

So as you can see in the bill, sections 16 to 18 outline the penalties reporting entities may face upon contravention. However, they do not require the funds to be extended to victims of modern slavery. Canadian modern slavery legislation similarly fails to provide for any such mechanism, and a report by the Faculty of Law at McGill University found that relying on reputational risk by requiring companies to report on whether they provide compensation for victims is in fact a poor replacement for a requirement that companies

provide such compensation. So I believe rather than having the penalties that companies face being kind of exemplary in nature or provided to the department for continuing costs, they should be maybe as such, but also to provide victims with money as well so that they can have a proper remedial kind of remediation and just they've felt justice.

Thank you. Was that all of your points- Yeah... to highlight today? Yes. That was everything. Thank you both. Look, really appreciate your contribution to this process, your written and your oral submission. We really appreciate it. Thank you. Great. We'll move on now to Redstone Consulting. Redstone, we have Brown Makatese submitting on behalf.

Welcome. We have 10 minutes with you this afternoon, that includes time for questions, and we'll hand over to you now. Good afternoon, and thank you for the opportunity to speak this afternoon. My name is Brown Makatese, and I'm the founder of Redstone Consulting. It's a business advisory firm.

So when people hear the words modern slavery, they often think of something extreme, obvious, and far away, something that happens somewhere else in very dramatic circumstances, and it's not something that happens in New Zealand. But the point I made in my submission is that it's not always that obvious. So serious exploitation, to me, can grow inside work arrangements that look normal from the outside.

It can also sit inside jobs that seem legal, ordinary, and part of everyday business, and it can happen where people are under pressure, where they do not feel safe speaking up, and where they depend heavily on the job to survive, and where responsibility is blurred, so nobody really takes ownership. And that is why I think we need to stop thinking about this as only a problem of a few bad actors.

We also need to think about the conditions that allow exploitation to happen in the first place. So for example, when workers have very little control over their hours, their bargaining power, their job security, and very little confidence that they can complain without losing income, that creates a risk. And when business models are set up in a way that pushes that responsibility further and further down through labor hire and contracting chains or platform-based work, that also creates the risk.

And when everyone in that chain can point to someone else and say, "Well, it's not really our responsibility," then the exploitation becomes easier to hide. That, in simple terms, is the concern behind my submission. So I'm not saying that every insecure job is modern slavery, and I'm not saying that every business is doing the wrong thing.

What I'm saying is that some work arrangements, they create the kind of environment where serious exploitation becomes more likely, and they become harder to detect or harder for workers to escape. So if we only step in once the worst harm has already happened, then we have left it too late. That is why I support a preventative approach.

To me, that means the law should do more than just respond to the damage that's done, and it should also expect organizations to look properly where the risks are and take the reasonable steps to reduce those risks and be accountable for what is happening in their operations and supply chains. So because if the law only works after a worker has already been harmed and after that worker has found the courage and safety to report it, then the system is putting too much weight on the most vulnerable person in that situation. And in many cases, that person is exactly the least

able to carry that burden. So I also want to make the point that reporting on paper is not enough. A business can publish a statement, they can write a policy, and still leave the underlying risk untouched. So if New Zealand wants to take this issue seriously, the answer cannot be just to ask organizations to describe the problem. The answer has to be to ask them what they're actually doing about it.

That is why I think a stronger due diligence approach matters, and not because businesses need punishment for its own sake, but because prevention needs to be built into the system. I also think this matters for good businesses. Responsible employers should not be undercut by business models that rely on insecurity, confusion, or shifting all the risk onto its workers.

So a stronger framework is not only about protecting vulnerable people, it's also about making sure that decent businesses are not competing against practices that should never have been acceptable in the first place. So my submission also points that New Zealand is not alone at looking at these issues. Other countries have already moved forward with their reporting and accountability frameworks, and the international direction is clearly toward expecting more from organizations when it comes to exploitation risk.

So I think we have a real opportunity. We can choose a weak model that mostly produces paperwork, or we can choose a model that's more practical, more preventative, and more focused on the workers. And the main message I want to leave with the committee today is that if we are serious about modern slavery and worker exploitation, that we cannot wait until the harm is obvious, severe, and already done, okay? We need a system that looks earlier, acts earlier, and protects people earlier. So that means clear expectations, real

accountability, safe ways for workers to raise concerns, and obligations that are strong enough to change behavior and practice, not just in theory. Thank you. I welcome any questions. Thank you very much, Brown. Appreciate your submission, your approach. We'll move on to our next submitter.

It doesn't appear that we have any questions for you. But not an indication of a lack of interest, just a very fulsome and comprehensive submission. Thank you. Thank you very much. And next we have Brian Ward.Welcome, Brian. Hello. Hello. We have five minutes with you this afternoon.

That includes time for questions. We'll hand over to you now. Okay. Thanks very much. Well done everybody doing this. It's great. How familiar is the committee with B Corp certification? Can I just ask the general question, just roughly, without asking everybody individually? Is there a familiarity with B Corp?

Sorry. Yes. Yep, you've just got us nodding. Most of us are on mute. Oh, okay. That's why. Great. I'll carry on. Yes. I'll just give you a couple of facts. USA have 36 million companies, only 2,500 are B Corp certified. New Zealand has 754,000 companies, only 180 of them are B Corp certified.

So, talk about open to unethical behavior. The USA Walk Free Global Slavery Index is four, and the New Zealand Walk Free Global Slavery Index is 1.8, so we're better on that front, too. B Corp reduces the need to have to tax to pay for company externalities resulting from clean-up, health and safety, and living wage, for example. And B Corp has initiatives similar to what the EU are working on, so there's an alignment there between B Corp and the EU provisions.

One thing that came up from what I listened to earlier was, how can whistleblower provisions be included in this legislation? It seemed to me that there's a need for that. Anyway, just back to the specific focus I have in my submission was, I believe the focus should be on importers and known unethical products.

That's where the focus should be when the energy is on this is done. And in my example, we could well be making the same material that is made in China, that is used-- We know most solar panels have forced labor or slave labor. We have unethical practices in the manufacture of our solar panels. Most of them coming into New Zealand will have an element of that, and we don't know much about it.

My company is looking to make the same stuff in New Zealand, but using ethical and modern practices with health and safety. We can't compete with slave or forced labor. I will make a point that some companies in this game protect their IP fiercely, and they won't let anybody near their operation.

So, there's that hurdle to get over, too. But high purity quartz technical knowledge largely resides in the Northern Hemisphere, and we don't know much about it here, but we can certainly have input into it from the point of view of helping the industry clean up its act.

And there's a significant opportunity for this and other things related to that to be carried out in New Zealand. So, I'll just conclude by saying the focus for me should be on importers and known unethical products. Thank you. Thank you very much, Brian. Just a quick question from myself.

When you said that the agent or the organizations refuse to let people in to see, are you sort of indicating that even if companies were going through their process, auditing their supply chain, there'd be some of their suppliers that they wouldn't be able to tell one way or the other?

Well, in general, this stuff travels around the world before it comes to us- Yes... a bit like some of those other products. They go from one country to another to get this and that done, and then they end up in a solar panel and then they come to- Mm... New Zealand. So, some of the earlier purification techniques are highly secretive, and the companies, they've got a monopoly on a lot of it, on this stuff- Mm... and for IP reasons, they won't let anybody in there.

Now, I don't know how much of that is involved as unethical behavior in amongst that-... but I'm just flagging it. Right. Okay. No, I really appreciate that. Thank you. Very helpful. Thanks, Brian. Appreciate your participation in the process- Okay. Thank you... and your submission. Thank you. We'll move on now to our next submitter. We have Jackie St.
John and Kylie Stevens speaking on behalf of Oceana Gold.

Welcome, Jackie. We have 10 minutes with you this afternoon. That includes time for questions. I can see there might be a flash- Sorry... of someone beside you. Perhaps that's Kylie. Having some technical difficulties. That's okay. Sorry. We can see you, but we cannot hear you. Ah. And you can see me, but you cannot see Kylie-... which is a bit of a problem.

You see the apparition of Kylie at times. One moment. I'll see if I canChange the background It's most im- There's a little better. No You have to take away the blue We can go with no background. One moment, please Background blur, that's your issue, but you can't hear me tell you that, so.

How's that? There we go. There we go. Can you hear us now? We still cannot hear you. Wonder- Okay. Try us again. I wonder if Clark can message you to just say it doesn't matter necessarily as long as we can hear you, which we can.
Goodness me.

Maybe I'll speak again to see if you can now hear me. Unfortunately, we can't hear you, and I'm very sorry about this because I'm trying to change micro and see whether that ... So the committee secretariat has hopefully just let you know that we can hear you, so if you can go for it, that's okay.

I'm working on that. Can hear something. Vaguely heard you then, I think. Oh, okay. Testing. Testing. Thank you. Oh, there we go. Sorry about that. No, that's okay. Really, you only have to hear us to understand the- Just-... restrictions which you already know, which is that you've got 10 minutes.

It includes time for questions and then hopefully some questions at the end. Wonderful. We'll hand over to you now. Thank you. We'll rip into it. Kia ora. Called Jacqui St John, toku ingoa. Legal manager for OceanaGold, and with me is my colleague Kylie Stevens. Thanks for the opportunity to present to you today. OceanaGold is New Zealand's largest gold producer, with operations at Macraes in Otago and Waihi in the Waikato, both of which have operated for over 36 years. We're part of the larger OceanaGold Corporation group, a multinational gold mining company with

operations in New Zealand, the United States, and the Philippines, and we're listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange and more recently, the New York Stock Exchange. OceanaGold supports the intent of this bill. The elimination of modern slavery and forced labor in global supply chains is a critical objective and one that OceanaGold is already actively engaged in addressing.

As a group, OceanaGold has been producing modern slavery statements since 2020 to achieve compliance with both Canadian and Australian modern slavery laws. The statements outline the measures that we have implemented to identify, assess, mitigate modern slavery risks across our global operations and supply chains.

We could provide you with an example of a modern slavery statement if you were interested to receive one. Our submission is about ensuring that the New Zealand regime achieves transparency and accountability in a way that is effective but practical and aligned with established international reporting frameworks.

We've made recommendations that we consider will improve the bill, and Kylie will introduce some of those to you. Our primary submission is to ensure that the New Zealand reporting regime is consistent with existing international reporting regimes. We think the bill should, one, allow reporting entities to report by reference to their own financial year.

That is not the single prescribed date for all entities being a non-government entity reporting period of 1st of April to 31st of March. OceanaGold modern slavery reporting draws from governance, risk, procurement, legal sustainability, and finance processes that are structured around our existing financial reporting cycle of a calendar year.

Reporting to a different period would add complexity, additional work burden, and extra reporting. Aligning to our existing reporting period would enable an easier bedding in on reporting. Two, recognize and align with equivalent overseas reporting and allow group-level reporting, including permitting reliance on modern slavery statements prepared under the Australian and Canadian frameworks where they contain materially equivalent information.

This reflects how multinational businesses actually manage modern slavery risks and would eliminate unnecessary duplication and additional costs to reporting entities in having to create a bespoke New Zealand report. Section 9 of the bill signals that there may be a mandatory prescribed form for statements. We ask that flexibility be provided for entities that are already reporting globally to depart from any prescribed form.

And three, include an appropriate transition period to enable entities to establish systems, governance, and reporting frameworks in an orderly and effective way. Currently, reports will be expected within six months of the end of the first reporting period. That is currently 31st of March. We suggest a change to allow a full reporting period to elapse once the act comes into effect, with reporting six months later.

Moving to a different submission point, OceanaGold is also interested to ensure that the proposed enforcement settings in the bill are proportionate to the obligations imposed by the bill. Our preference is that Section 17, director and officer criminal liability is removed from the bill.

However, if it is to remain, it's very important that New Zealand enforcement provisions are appropriately calibrated. We'd recommend a staged approach to implementation and a more enabling enforcement toolkit to allow a hierarchy of responses, ranging from warning to infringement penalty to finally, prosecution.Like the Australian and Canadian regimes, the New Zealand regime could recognize and provide and encourage good faith efforts to comply and provide an opportunity to correct inaccurate reporting, particularly in the early stages of the

regime. We consider director and officer liability should be reserved for more serious misconduct, not just procedural non-compliance with reporting obligations. So serious misconduct would be failures to report, repeated non-compliances, or deliberately misleading statements.

Currently, Section 17B creates a criminal offense where a director or officer knew or could reasonably be expected to have known that the offense was to be or was being committed and failed to take all reasonable steps to prevent or stop it. This is quite a far-reaching provision when a global supply chain is being considered.

We ask that consideration be given to introducing a defense if a director or officer can demonstrate that they have taken reasonable steps to prevent offending. This would cover a scenario where reporting procedures have been implemented and maintained, but the director was not reasonably aware of specific offending or the probability of it occurring.

So in conclusion, OceanaGold supports the intent of the Modern Slavery Bill, submits that alignment with established international frameworks would result in a more effective and practical regime for globally operating businesses like ours. Alignment will enhance transparency, ensure that New Zealand's regulatory framework is credible and capable of delivering its intended outcomes.

Thank you for your time. We are available to answer questions if there's any time for any. Thank you very much. Marginal, but we have one question, so we'll try and make that one quick. Thank you. Thank you so much for your submission. Just really quickly, just wanting to clarify two points in your submission. So I got the point around the transitional provisions, which I know is in your written submission too. But just to clarify, so at the moment it would be six months, but you're asking for it to be a full additional reporting year on top of that?

Yeah. So the first report would be due- 18 months, effectively Yeah, 18 months After the implementation of the act to enable a full cycle of gathering of information to be available to the business and also to enable reporting systems and mechanisms to be established and set up for those who don't already have them.

Thanks. And just the other thing was in relation to the penalties. We understand this is something that Australia is looking at. So in terms of the comparative model that you suggested, would you imagine that warnings to start with would be the first step, but envisioning a regime that did have penalties in place like- Yes, absolutely... we understand Australia is moving to.

Absolutely. So a hierarchy of proportionate response, and we see that in other legislation, and it could be mirrored here. Thanks. Thank you. Thank you both, and really appreciate your submission, both written and oral. Thank you for your understanding. And making your technology work.

I had technical difficulties. All good. We got there. Brilliant. Thank you. We'll move on now to our next submission. We have Rebekah Armstrong and Evelyn Painter representing World Vision New Zealand.

Kia ora. My name is Evelyn Painter. I am 12 years old. I am a World Vision advocacy ambassador, and I'm here today to represent the voices of children and young people across Aotearoa, New Zealand, who have spoken up about modern slavery. At World Vision's Annual Youth Conference, we worked alongside young people aged 10 to 18 to learn about modern slavery and the risks hidden in the products we buy every day.

Together, we helped collate 942 submissions to Parliament in support of this Modern Slavery Bill. Scarlet from Glen Eden Intermediate wrote, "Child labor shouldn't be supported. Modern slavery is horrible, and it is not fair." More than 12 million children are trapped in modern slavery, one in four victims globally. This is an issue that children and young people in Aotearoa feel deeply. For many young people, modern slavery has become personal, through stories, campaigns, research, and the voices of people who are too often unheard.

One young person, Vadika from Mount Albert Grammar School, wrote, "That person sitting on their shared mattress on the ground, working 12 hours a day and getting only a few cents could have very well been me. That immigrant who was promised a better life but got exploited instead could have been my mom, and that shatters my heart.

Treat humans like humans, not machines. I don't want my products to be made by someone who is suffering." Some of the key messages I heard were a need for greater accountability from businesses, the impacts of exploitation, and the belief that all humans deserve to be treated fairly and with dignity. Many of the young people who made submissions will spend decades living with the decisions made here as consumers, workers, business leaders, voters, and members of their communities. But this bill is not only about the future, it is about

now. Any modern slavery legislation passed by this Parliament must recognize that children are among the most vulnerable to exploitation, and that their experiences, rights, and protection needs to be at the center of law. Young people across Aotearoa have written letters, signed petitions, met with MPs, learnt about supply chains, and made submissions because they believe their voices can make a differenceThis bill reaching Parliament has shown that advocacy does work, and the next step is ensuring that this parliamentary process continues to listen to children and young

The Crown knows these carers simply cannot stop. They cannot resign. They cannot walk away.

The Crown also knows that in many, if not most cases, there is nobody else available to provide the care and nowhere suitable for their family member to go. That is precisely why this issue belongs before this committee. The Modern Slavery Bill recognizes that exploitation is not confined to criminal gangs, locked doors, or physical restraints. Its concepts of exploitation and servitude recognizes that labor can be extracted where a person cannot realistically cease providing it because of the consequences that would follow. Exploitation exists where

dependency, vulnerability, and unequal power remove any genuine freedom of choice. That description reflects the reality faced by many family carers in New ZealandToday, the Crown determines eligibility for disability support. The Crown determines funding. The Crown determines the level of support required, often acknowledging that is 24/7.

The Crown controls access to services, audits arrangements, and controls the entire system. Yet when carers seek recognition under the law already in place, namely the Employment Relations Act, the Crown denies responsibility. The Crown has sought to portray the Supreme Court decision recently handed down in Fleming as judicial interference with government policy. That is plain wrong.

The Supreme Court did not interfere with the policy. It simply applied the law to the reality of the arrangements the Crown had created and operated. The court confirmed that government policy, like any other policy, remains subject to the law. I note in this case, that law was the Employment Relations Act.

The Supreme Court did nothing more than uphold the rule of law in that matter. The principle is also central to this bill. The law must look beyond labels, structures, and legal fictions to the reality of power, control, dependency, and work. When the Supreme Court did exactly that, the Crown's response has been not compliance.

Its response is to introduce a Disability Support Services bill to remove the legal consequences of the court's findings. That should concern every member of this committee. Your role is not merely to process legislation. It is to scrutinize evidence, identify inconsistencies, and ensure Parliament does not apply one standard to private actors and another to itself.

The question here, members, is simple. If a private organization relied upon decades of continuous labor, denied meaningful employment protections, depended upon emotional obligation and family vulnerability to ensure that labor continued, and then sought legislative protection when challenged in the courts, would Parliament hesitate to describe that arrangement as exploitative? If the answer is no, and it should be, then the Crown should not be exempt from the same scrutiny. Parliament cannot credibly condemn exploitation in one bill while legislating to

preserve arrangements exhibiting many of the same characteristics in another. My submission is straightforward. New Zealand's disability support system has become dependent upon a level of unpaid and under-recognized family labor that, if imposed by anyone other than the state, would attract precisely the scrutiny this bill is intended to provide.

I therefore ask this committee to use its influence with the Social Services and Community Committee to ensure that the Disability Support Services Bill receives the rigorous scrutiny it presently lacks. Parliament should not be asked to endorse legislation that removes rights confirmed by the Supreme Court while simultaneously advancing legislation intended to combat exploitation in all its forms. Our recommendation is not that New Zealand simply copy Australia. Far from it. In simple terms, one high-quality statement should be capable of satisfying multiple regimes, provided it covers the right things. This is not to emphasize a lower standard approach. It should also, where possible, provide a clear safe harbor allowing entities to withhold or anonymize or aggregate or in fact delay disclosure

where that publication would be unlawful, unsafe, compromise an investigation, endanger individuals, or conflict with financial crime, privacy, or enforcement law obligations. This should not excuse non-reporting, to be clear. It should protect the way sensitive information is disclosed, not whether serious issues are reported.

This is particularly important for regulated sectors. For example, the financial sector and financial institutions in particular, where it may include anti-money laundering, counter-terrorism financing, sanctions, privacy, law enforcement, or tiffing-off restrictions that they are bound by.

A third and final point is that implementation should be practical. Businesses need clear rules on reporting periods, group statements, first reporting deadlines, transition arrangements, and guidance. We recommend allowing entities to report on their ordinary financial year or an approved 12-month reporting period.

The core test should be whether the bill directs effort towards better identification, prevention, mitigation, and remediation of modern slavery. The regime should also reward companies that find and fix problems, not companies that avoid looking.
